questions.question: Define the Multi-Armed Bandit problem and explain the explore-exploitation tradeoff with an example.
keyPoints: Define MAB: K arms with unknown reward distributions, T rounds, maximize cumulative reward
Explain explore: trying different arms to learn reward probabilities
Explain exploit: choosing arm with highest known expected reward
Tradeoff: balancing both to minimize regret
Example: Casino with slot machines, clinical trials, or online advertising

questions.question: Explain the epsilon-greedy algorithm with its advantages and limitations.
keyPoints: Algorithm: with prob ε explore random, with prob 1-ε exploit best-known arm
How Q-values are estimated and updated
Advantages: simple to implement, no parameter tuning beyond ε
Limitations: doesn't distinguish between well-explored and poorly-explored arms, fixed exploration rate may be suboptimal

questions.question: Derive and explain the Upper Confidence Bound (UCB) formula used in multi-armed bandits.
keyPoints: UCB formula: UCB(a) = Q(a) + c × √(ln(t) / N(a))
First term Q(a) is exploitation (estimated mean reward)
Second term is exploration bonus, decreases as N(a) increases
ln(t) ensures bonus grows with time (we're more uncertain overall)
No parameter tuning needed, theoretically optimal regret

questions.question: Compare epsilon-greedy, UCB, and Thompson Sampling algorithms.
keyPoints: Epsilon-greedy: simple, random exploration with fixed probability
UCB: deterministic bonus based on confidence, theoretical guarantees
Thompson Sampling: Bayesian posterior sampling, probabilistic exploration
Key differences in exploration approach and computational complexity
